# Page 1^page=1
Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition, Chapter 4: Threads

# Page 2^page=2
4.2 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Chapter 4: Threads uOverview uMultithreading Models uThread Libraries uExamples(Java Threads, OSs Threads) uThreading Issues

# Page 3^page=3
4.3 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Objectives u To introduce the notion of a thread — a fundamental unit of CPU utilization that forms the basis of multithreaded computer systems u To discuss the APIs for the Pthreads, Win32, and Java thread libraries u To examine issues related to multithreaded programming

# Page 4^page=4
4.4 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Review: Processes u The unit of execution and scheduling u A task created by the OS, running in a restricted virtual machine environment –a virtual CPU, virtual memory environment, interface to the OS via system calls u Sequential, instruction-at-a-time execution of a program. u Abstraction used for protection Ø Main Memory State (contents of Address Space) u Multiprogramming: overlap IO and CPU u Context Switches are expensive New Ready Running Exit Waiting admitted interrupt I/ O o r ev en t w a itI/O or eventcompletion dispatch done Yield

# Page 5^page=5
4.5 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Review: Processes u How does parent know child process has finished if exec does not return? main(int argc, char **argv) { char *myName = argv[1]; char *progName = argv[2]; int cpid = fork(); if (cpid == 0) { printf(“The child of %s is %d\n”, myName, getpid()); execlp(“/bin/ls”,“ls”,NULL); printf(“OH NO. THEY LIED TO ME!!!\n”); } else { printf(“My child is %d\n”, cpid); wait(cpid); exit(0); } }

# Page 6^page=6
4.6 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Review: Cooperating Processes u Processes can be independent or work cooperatively u Cooperating processes can be used: Ø to gain speedup by overlapping activities or working in parallel Ø to better structure an application as set of cooperating processes Ø to share information between jobs u Sometimes processes are structured as a pipeline Ø each produces work for the next stage that consumes it main() read_data() for(all data) compute(); CreateProcess(write_data()); endfor main() read_data() for(all data) compute(); write_data(); endfor

# Page 7^page=7
4.7 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Consider the following code fragment on a dual core CPU for(k = 0; k < n; k++) a[k] = b[k] * c[k] + d[k] * e[k]; Instead: CreateProcess(fn, 0, n/2); CreateProcess(fn, n/2, n); fn(l, m) for(k = l; k < m; k++) a[k] = b[k] * c[k] + d[k] * e[k]; Case for Parallelism

# Page 8^page=8
4.8 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Case for Parallelism n Consider a Web server: create a number of process, and for each process do Ø get network message from client Ø get URL data from disk Ø compose response Ø send response u Server connections are fast, but client connections may not be (grandma’s modem connection) Ø Takes server a loooong time to feed the response to grandma Ø While it’s doing that it can’t service any more requests(进程占用资源)

# Page 9^page=9
4.9 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Parallel Programs u To build parallel programs, such as: Ø Parallel execution on a multiprocessor Ø Web server to handle multiple simultaneous web requests u We will need to: Ø Create several processes that can execute in parallel Ø Cause each to map to the same address space 4 because they’re part of the same computation Ø Give each its starting address and initial parameters Ø The OS will then schedule these processes in parallel

# Page 10^page=10
4.10 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Processes Overheads u A full process includes numerous things: Ø an address space (defining all the code and data pages) Ø OS resources and accounting information Ø a “thread of control”, 4 defines where the process is currently executing 4 That is the PC and registers u Creating a new process is costly Ø all of the structures (e.g., page tables) that must be allocated u Communicating between processes is costly Ø most communication goes through the OS

# Page 11^page=11
4.11 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Need “Lightweight” Processes u What’s similar in these processes? Ø They all share the same code and data (address space) Ø They all share the same privileges Ø They share almost everything in the process u What don’t they share? Ø Each has its own PC, registers, and stack pointer u Idea: why don’t we separate the idea of process (address space, accounting, etc.) from that of the minimal “thread of control” (PC, SP, registers)?按执行线索分离

# Page 12^page=12
4.12 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Threads and Processes u Most operating systems therefore support two entities: Ø the process: which defines the address space and general process attributes Ø the thread: which defines a sequential execution stream within a process u A thread is bound to a single process: For each process, however, there may be many threads. u Threads are the unit of scheduling, Processes are containers in which threads execute

# Page 13^page=13
4.13 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Threads vs. Processes u A thread has no data segment or heap u A thread cannot live on its own, it must live within a process u There can be more than one thread in a process, the first thread calls main & has the process’s stack u Inexpensive creation u Inexpensive context switching u If a thread dies, its stack is reclaimed u A process has code/data/heap & other segments u There must be at least one thread in a process u Threads within a process share code/data/heap, share I/O, but each has its own stack & registers u Expensive creation u Expensive context switching u If a process dies, its resources are reclaimed & all threads die

# Page 14^page=14
4.14 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Separating Threads and Processes u Makes it easier to support multithreaded applications Ø Different from multiprocessing, multiprogramming, multitasking u Concurrency (multithreading) is useful for: Ø improving program structure Ø handling concurrent events (e.g., web requests) Ø building parallel programs Ø Resource sharing Ø Multiprocessor utilization u Is multithreading useful even on a single processor? Benefits: Ø Responsiveness Ø Resource Sharing Ø Economy Ø Scalability

# Page 15^page=15
4.15 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Multicore Programming u Multicore systems putting pressure on programmers, challenges include Ø Dividing activities Ø Balance Ø Data splitting Ø Data dependency Ø Testing and debugging

# Page 16^page=16
4.16 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Multithreaded Server Architecture

# Page 17^page=17
4.17 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Parallel Execution on a Multicore System Concurrent Execution on a Single-core System S=1/(1-a+a/n)S=1/(1-a+a/n)

# Page 18^page=18
4.18 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Amdahl’s Law（阿姆达尔定律） u S is serial portion， A is parallel portion，N processing cores Speedup=1/(1-A+A/N) or u That is, if application is 75% parallel / 25% serial, moving from 1 to 2 cores results in speedup of 1.6 times u As N approaches infinity, speedup approaches 1 / S Serial portion of an application has disproportionate effect on performance gained by adding additional cores

# Page 19^page=19
4.19 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Implementation of thread There are actually 2 level of threads: u Kernel threads: Ø Supported and managed directly by the kernel. u User threads: Ø Supported above the kernel, and without kernel knowledge.

# Page 20^page=20
4.20 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Kernel Threads u Also called Lightweight Processes (LWP) u Kernel threads still suffer from performance problems u Operations on kernel threads are slow because: Ø a thread operation still requires a system call Ø kernel threads may be overly general 4to support needs of different users, languages, etc. Ø the kernel doesn’t trust the user 4there must be lots of checking on kernel calls

# Page 21^page=21
4.21 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition User-Level Threads u For speed, implement threads at the user level u A user-level thread is managed by the run-time system Ø user-level code that is linked with your program u Each thread is represented simply by: Ø PC Ø Registers Ø Stack Ø Small control block u All thread operations are at the user-level: Ø Creating a new thread Ø switching between threads Ø synchronizing between threads

# Page 22^page=22
4.22 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition User-Level Threads u User-level threads Ø the thread scheduler is part of a library, outside the kernel Ø thread context switching and scheduling is done by the library Ø Can either use cooperative or pre-emptive threads 4 cooperative threads are implemented by: – CreateThread(), DestroyThread(), Yield(), Suspend(), etc. 4 pre-emptive threads are implemented with a timer (signal) – where the timer handler decides which thread to run next

# Page 23^page=23
4.23 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Example User Thread Interface ut = thread_fork(initial context): create a new thread of control; uthread_stop(): stop the calling thread, sometimes called thread_block; uthread_start(t): start the named thread; uthread_yield(): voluntarily give up the processor; uthread_exit(): terminate the calling thread, sometimes called thread_destroy;

# Page 24^page=24
4.24 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Key Data Structures your process address space your program: for i (1, 10, I++) thread_fork(I); …. user-level thread code: proc thread_fork()… proc thread_block()… proc thread_exit()... queue of thread control blocks per-thread stacks your data (shared by all your threads):

# Page 25^page=25
4.25 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Multiplexing User-Level Threads u The user-level thread package sees a “virtual” processor(s) Ø it schedules user-level threads on these virtual processors Ø each “virtual” processor is implemented by a kernel thread u The big picture Ø Create as many kernel threads as there are processors Ø Create as many user-level threads as the application needs Ø Multiplex user-level threads on top of the kernel-level threads u Why not just create as many kernel-level threads as app needs? Ø Context switching Ø Resources

# Page 26^page=26
4.26 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition User-Level vs. Kernel Threads User-Level Ø Managed by application Ø Kernel not aware of thread Ø Context switching cheap Ø Create as many as needed Ø Must be used with care Kernel-Level Ø Managed by kernel Ø Consumes kernel resources Ø Context switching expensive Ø Number limited by kernel resources Ø Simpler to use Key issue: kernel threads provide virtual processors to user-level threads, but if all of kthreads block, then all user-level threads will block even if the program logic allows them to proceed

# Page 27^page=27
4.27 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition User/Kernel Threads u Thread management done by user-level threads library u Three primary thread libraries: Ø POSIX Pthreads Ø Win32 threads Ø Java threads u Supported by the Kernel u Examples Ø Windows XP/2000 Ø Solaris Ø Linux Ø Tru64 UNIX Ø Mac OS X

# Page 28^page=28
4.28 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Multithreading Models uMany-to-One uOne-to-One uMany-to-Many

# Page 29^page=29
4.29 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Many-to-One Model Thread creation, scheduling, synchronization done in user space.Mainly used in language systems, portable libraries. uFast - no system calls required uFew system dependencies; portable uNo parallel execution of threads - can’t exploit multiple CPUs uAll threads block when one uses synchronous I/O Many user-level threads mapped to single kernel thread

# Page 30^page=30
4.30 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition One-to-one Model u More concurrency u Better multiprocessor performance u Each user thread requires creation of kernel thread u Each thread requires kernel resources; limits number of total threads Thread creation, scheduling, synchronization require system calls. Used in Linux Threads, Windows NT, Windows 2000, OS/2, Solaris 9 and later Each user-level thread maps to kernel thread

# Page 31^page=31
4.31 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Many-to-Many Model If U<k? No benefits of multithreading If U>k, some threads may have to wait for an Kthread to run • Active thread - executing on an Kthread • Runnable thread - waiting for an Kthread A thread gives up control of Kthread under the following: – synchronization, lower priority, yielding, time slicing nAllows many user level threads to be mapped to many kernel threads. nExamples: Solaris, Windows NT/2000 with the ThreadFiber package

# Page 32^page=32
4.32 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Two-level Model n Supports both bound and unbound threads l Bound threads - permanently mapped to a single, dedicated kthread l Unbound threads - may move among kthreads in set n Thread creation, scheduling, synchronization done in user space n Flexible approach, “best of both worlds” (两全其美) Used in ,Solaris 8 and earlier implementation of Pthreads and several other Unix implementations (IRIX, HP-UX, Tru64 UNIX) Similar to M:M, except that it allows a user thread to be bound to(绑定) kernel thread

# Page 33^page=33
4.33 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Thread Libraries u Thread library provides programmer with API for creating and managing threads u Two primary ways of implementing Ø Library entirely in user space Ø Kernel-level library supported by the OS u Examples: Ø Java thread Ø Win32 threads Ø POSIX Pthreads

# Page 34^page=34
4.34 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Java Threads u Java threads are managed by the JVM, typically implemented using the threads model provided by underlying OS: Ø On windows system, java threads are implemented using Win32 API Ø On UNIX or Linux, use Pthreads u Java Threads包中定义了Thread类和Runnable接口,两种创建方法: ① 扩展Thread类，并重置它的RUN()方法; ② 定义一个类，实现Runnable接口；

# Page 35^page=35
4.35 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Extending the Thread Class class Worker1 extends Thread { public void run() { System.out.println(“I am a Worker Thread”); } } public class First { public static void main(String args[]) { Worker runner = new Worker1(); runner.start(); System.out.println(“I am the main thread”); } } start() 为新线程分配内存并初始化，然后调用run()方法

# Page 36^page=36
4.36 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition The Runnable Interface public interface Runnable { public abstract void run(); } class Worker2 implements Runnable { public void run() {/*定义类Worker2实现Runnable接口,定义run()方法*/ System.out.println(“I am a Worker Thread”); } } public class Second { public static void main(String args[]) { Runnable runner = new Worker2(); Thread thrd = new Thread(runner); thrd.start(); System.out.println(“I am the main thread”); } /* 创建线程对象传递给Runnable对象，新线程由start()方法创建后， 开始执行Runnable对象的run()方法*/ }

# Page 37^page=37
4.37 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Java Thread States

# Page 38^page=38
4.38 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Win32 Threads  Win32 API is the primary API for Microsoft OS (Win95,98,NT,2000,XP ),A kernel-level library on windows systems Windows XP Threads:  Implements the one-to-one mapping, kernel-level  Each thread contains  A thread id  Register set  Separate user and kernel stacks  Private data storage area  The register set, stacks, and private storage area are known as the context of the threads

# Page 39^page=39
4.39 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Windows XP Threads  The primary data structures of a thread include:  ETHREAD (executive thread block)  KTHREAD (kernel thread block)  TEB (thread environment block)

# Page 40^page=40
4.40 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Process Control Block (PCB) Program Counter Parent PID … Handle Table Process ID (PID) Registers Next Process Block Image File Name PCB List of Thread Control Blocks List of open files …  This is an abstract view  Windows implementation of PCB is split in multiple data structures Next TCB … Thread Control Block (TCB)

# Page 41^page=41
4.41 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Windows Process and Thread Internals Process environment block Thread environment block Process block (EPROCESS) Thread block (ETHREAD) Win32 process block Handle table ... Process address space System address space KTHREAD 线程地址等 调度信息等

# Page 42^page=42
4.42 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Windows Processes & Threads Internal Data Structures Process Object Handle Table VAD VAD VAD object object Virtual Address Space Descriptors Access Token Thread Thread Thread . . . Access Token Files, share memory section, synchronizati on objects identification and credentials

# Page 43^page=43
4.43 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Windows XP Threads init ready waiting running terminated scheduler dispatch waiting for I/O or event I/O or event completion interrupt quantum expired admitted exit

# Page 44^page=44
4.44 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition CPU Switch from Thread to Thread Thread T1 executing executing ready or waiting Save state into TCB2 Reload state from TCB1 Save state into TCB1 Reload state from TCB2 Interrupt or system call Thread T2 executing Interrupt or system call ready or waiting ready or waiting

# Page 45^page=45
4.45 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition POSIX Pthreads u May be provided either as user-level or kernel-level u A POSIX standard (IEEE 1003.1c) API for thread creation and synchronization u API specifies behavior of the thread library, implementation is up to development of the library u Common in UNIX operating systems (Linux, Solaris, Mac OS X)

# Page 46^page=46
4.46 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Linux Threads u Linux does not distinguish between processes and threads; Linux refers to them as tasks rather than process or thread; u Thread creation is done through system call clone() or pthread_create() ; u调用fork()创建一个新进程,它具有父进程所有相关数据结构的拷 贝； u调用clone()创建新进程,但它不复制父进程的数据结构,而是指向 了父进程的数据结构,从而允许子进程共享父进程的内存和其他资 源。或者定义一个函数作为线程的入口，然后调用 pthread_create() 创建线程。

# Page 47^page=47
4.47 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Linux Threads u clone() allows a child task to share the address space of the parent task (process) u Flags determine how much sharing is to take place between the parent and child u If no flag is set when clone(), no sharing take place, resulting in functionality similar to fork()

# Page 48^page=48
4.48 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Solaris threads u Solaris is a version of UNIX with support for threads at the kernel and user levels, symmetric multiprocessing, and real-time scheduling. Ø Kernel threads； Ø Lightweight Processes; Ø User Level Threads； u LWP – (lightweight processes)intermediate level between user-level threads and kernel-level threads.

# Page 49^page=49
4.49 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Solaris threads u Kernel Threads： small data structure and a stack; thread switching relatively fast. Kernel only sees the LWPs that support user-level threads. u LWP：a register set for the user-thread it is running, accounting and memory information, switching between LWPs is relatively slow. u User Threads：only need stack and program counter; no kernel involvement means fast switching. Without LWPs, user threads would contend(竞争) at system call；

# Page 50^page=50
4.50 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition u User level threads may be either bound or unbound； u Bound: A user thread is permanently attached to a LWP; u All unbound threads in an application are multiplexed onto the pool of available for the application; Ø Threads are unbound by default . u The thread library adjusts LWPs in the pool  The thread library ages LWPs and deletes them when they are unused for a long time, typically about 5 minutes. Solaris threads

# Page 51^page=51
4.51 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Timeslice or Preempt Wakeup Stop Blocking System Call WakeupDispatch Runnable Running Active Stopped Continue Stop Stop Stop Sleep Dispatch Stop WakeupContinue Preempt Stopped Runnable Active Sleeping 用户级线程 轻权进程Solaris: User Threads and LWP

# Page 52^page=52
4.52 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition C library functions on Solaris threads •Create a User Threads • int thr_create(void *stack_base, size_t stack_size, void *(*start_routine)(void *), void *arg, long flags, thread_t *new_thread_id); flags：THR_BOUND（永久捆绑） THR_NEW_LWP（创建新LWP放入LWP池） 两者同时指定则创建两个新LWP，一个永久捆绑而另一个放入LWP池； •Create a LWP int _lwp_create(ucontext_t *contextp, unsigned long flags, lwpid_t *new_lwp_id); • Make a context on LWP • void _lwp_makecontext(ucontext_t *ucp, void (*start_routine)( void *), void *arg, void *private, caddr_t stack_base, size_t stack_size);

# Page 53^page=53
4.53 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Implicit Threading u Growing in popularity as numbers of threads increase, program correctness more difficult with explicit threads u Creation and management of threads done by compilers and run- time libraries rather than programmers u Three methods explored Ø Thread Pools Ø OpenMP Ø Grand Central Dispatch u Other methods include Microsoft Threading Building Blocks (TBB), java.util.concurrent package

# Page 54^page=54
4.54 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Thread Pools u Create a number of threads in a pool where they await work u Advantages: Ø Usually slightly faster to service a request with an existing thread than create a new thread Ø Allows the number of threads in the application(s) to be bound to the size of the pool Ø Separating task to be performed from mechanics of creating task allows different strategies for running task(执行与创建分离) 4 i.e.Tasks could be scheduled to run periodically u Windows API supports thread pools:

# Page 55^page=55
4.55 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition OpenMP u Set of compiler directives and an API for C, C++, FORTRAN u Provides support for parallel programming in shared-memory environments u Identifies parallel regions – blocks of code that can run in parallel #pragma omp parallel Create as many threads as there are cores #pragma omp parallel for for(i=0;i<N;i++) { c[i] = a[i] + b[i]; } Run for loop in parallel

# Page 56^page=56
4.56 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Grand Central Dispatch u Apple technology for Mac OS X and iOS operating systems u Extensions to C, C++ languages, API, and run-time library u Allows identification of parallel sections u Manages most of the details of threading u Block is in “^{ }” - ˆ{ printf("I am a block"); } u Blocks placed in dispatch queue Ø Assigned to available thread in thread pool when removed from queue

# Page 57^page=57
4.57 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Grand Central Dispatch u Two types of dispatch queues: Ø serial – blocks removed in FIFO order, queue is per process, called main queue 4Programmers can create additional serial queues within program Ø concurrent – removed in FIFO order but several may be removed at a time 4Three system wide queues with priorities low, default, high

# Page 58^page=58
4.58 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Threading Issues u Semantics of fork() and exec() system calls u Thread cancellation of target thread Ø Asynchronous or deferred u Signal handling u Thread pools u Thread-specific data u Scheduler activations

# Page 59^page=59
4.59 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition Thread Hazards int a = 1, b = 2, w = 2; main() { CreateThread(fn, 4); CreateThread(fn, 4); while(w) ; } fn() { int v = a + b; w--; } n A statement like “w--” in C (or C++) is implemented by several machine instructions: ld r4, #w add r4, r4, -1 st r4, #w n Now, imagine the following sequence, what is the value of w? ld r4, #w ______________ ______________ ______________ add r4, r4, -1 st r4, #w ______________ ld r4, #w add r4, r4, -1 st r4, #w

# Page 60^page=60
4.60 Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition 作业 n 编写一个C/S架构的分布式程序, Server接收Client发来 的请求,执行一个计算F(X)并给Client返回结果;分别用进 程与线程作为服务器Server实现,并比较服务器的开销.可 以在一台机器上模拟.

# Page 61^page=61
Silberschatz, Galvin and Gagne ©2009Operating System Concepts – 8th Edition, End of Chapter 4
